Before changing any motherboard jumper settings, turn OFF the computer and unplug the power cord.

1. Clear RTC RAM (2-pin CLRTC)

This header allows you to clear the Real Time Clock (RTC) RAM in CMOS. You can clear the CMOS memory of date, time, and system setup parameters by erasing the CMOS RTC RAM data.

<PIC>

To erase the RTC RAM:
1. Turn OFF the computer and unplug the power cord.
2. Use a metal object such as a screwdriver to short the two pins.
3. Plug the power cord and turn ON the computer.
4. Hold down the < Del > key during the boot process and enter BIOS setup to reenter data.

If the steps above do not help, remove the onboard battery and short the two pins again to clear the CMOS RTC RAM data. After clearing the CMOS, reinstall the battery.

2. CPU Over Voltage jumper (3-pin CPU_OV)

The CPU Over Voltage jumper allows you to set a higher CPU voltage for a flexible overclocking system, depending on the type of the installed CPU.

<PIC>

CPU_OV setting:
- Pins 1-2: Disable, default setting
- Pins 2-3: Enable

To gain more CPU voltage setting, insert the jumper to pins 2-3. To go back to its default CPU voltage setting, insert the jumper to pins 1-2.

Note: You do not need to clear the RTC when the system hangs due to overclocking. For system failure due to overclocking, shut down and reboot the system; the BIOS automatically resets parameter settings to default values through CPU Parameter Recall (C.P.R.).,['Manual25_33', 'Manual25_34']

7. Line In port (light blue). This port connects to the tape, CD, DVD player, or other audio sources.  
8. Line Out port (lime). This port connects to a headphone or a speaker. In the 4.1, 5.1, and 7.1 channel configurations, the function of this port becomes Front Speaker Out.  
9. Microphone port (pink). This port connects to a microphone.  

Audio 2, 4.1, 5.1, or 7.1-channel configuration:

<PIC>

10. Optical S/PDIF out port. This port allows you to connect your PC to amplified speakers, headphones, or S/PDIF compliant devices.  
11. USB 3.1 Type A port. This 9-pin USB Type A port is for USB 3.1 devices.  
12. USB 3.1 Type C port. This USB Type C port is for USB 3.1 mobile or peripheral devices.  
13. USB 3.0 ports 3~6. These 9-pin USB ports are for USB 3.0 devices.  
14. DVI-D port. This port is for any DVI-D compatible device.  
15. HDMI port. This port is for a High-Definition Multimedia Interface (HDMI) connector, and is HDCP compliant.

You can update the motherboard BIOS file using EZ Update, EZ Flash 3, or BIOS Updater.

1. Using EZ Update in AI Suite 3

EZ Update is a utility that allows you to automatically update your motherboard’s softwares, drivers and the BIOS version easily. With this utility, you can also manually update the saved BIOS and select a boot logo when the system goes into POST.

To launch EZ Update, click EZ Update on the AI Suite 3 main menu bar.

<PIC>

Note: EZ Update requires an Internet connection either through a network or an ISP.

2. Using EZ Flash 3 in BIOS

EZ Flash 3 allows you to update the BIOS without using an OS-based utility.

<PIC>

To update the BIOS using EZ Flash 3:
1. Enter Advanced Mode of the BIOS setup program.
2. Go to the Tool menu, select EZ Flash 3 Utility, and press Enter.
3. Choose to update by USB or by Internet.

Via USB:
1. Insert the USB flash disk that contains the latest BIOS file to the USB port, then select by USB.
2. Press Tab to switch to the Drive field.
3. Use the Up/Down arrow keys to find the USB flash disk, then press Enter.
4. Press Tab to switch to the Folder Info field.
5. Select the BIOS file and press Enter to perform the BIOS update.

Via the Internet:
1. Select by Internet.
2. Use the Left/Right arrow keys to select an Internet connection method, then press Enter.
3. Follow the onscreen instructions to complete the update.

Reboot the system when the update process is done.

3. Using BIOS Updater in DOS environment

BIOS Updater allows you to update the BIOS in DOS environment.

Before updating BIOS:
• Prepare the motherboard support DVD and a USB flash drive.
• Download the latest BIOS file and BIOS Updater and save them in your USB flash drive.
• Ensure that your USB flash drive is in single partition and in FAT32/16 format.
• Turn off the computer.
• Ensure that your computer has a DVD optical drive.

Booting the system in DOS environment:
1. Insert the USB flash drive with the latest BIOS file and BIOS Updater to the USB port.
2. Boot your computer then press F8 to launch the select boot device screen.
3. Insert the Support DVD into the optical drive then select the optical drive as the boot device.

<PIC>

4. When the booting message appears, press Enter within five seconds to enter FreeDOS prompt.
5. On the FreeDOS prompt, type d: and press Enter to switch from the optical drive to the USB flash drive.

Updating the BIOS file:
1. On the FreeDOS prompt, type bupdater /pc /g and press Enter.
2. On the BIOS Updater screen, press Tab to switch from Files panel to Drives panel then select D:.

<PIC>

3. Press Tab to switch from Drives panel to Files panel, select the BIOS file, and press Enter.
4. After the BIOS Updater checks the selected BIOS file, select Yes to confirm the BIOS update.

<PIC>

5. Select Yes then press Enter. When BIOS update is done, press Esc to exit BIOS Updater.
6. Restart your computer.

Important notes:
• Save a copy of the original motherboard BIOS file to a USB flash disk in case you need to restore the BIOS in the future.
• EZ Flash 3 supports USB flash disks with FAT32/16 format and single partition only.
• DO NOT shut down or reset the system while updating the BIOS to prevent system boot failure.
• Ensure to load the BIOS default settings to ensure system compatibility and stability. Select Load Optimized Defaults under the Exit BIOS menu.,['Manual25_60', 'Manual25_61', 'Manual25_62', 'Manual25_63', 'Manual25_64']

To prepare and begin installing the CPU on this motherboard:

<PIC>

This motherboard comes with a surface mount LGA1151 socket designed for 6th Generation processors.

CPU socket LGA1151

Unplug all power cables before installing the CPU.

Important precautions:
• Ensure that you install the correct CPU designed for the LGA1151 socket only. DO NOT install a CPU designed for LGA1150, LGA1155 and LGA1156 sockets on the LGA1151 socket.  
• Upon purchase of the motherboard, ensure that the PnP cap is on the socket and the socket contacts are not bent. Contact your retailer immediately if the PnP cap is missing, or if you see any damage to the PnP cap/socket contacts/motherboard components.  
• Keep the cap after installing the motherboard. It will process Return Merchandise Authorization (RMA) requests only if the motherboard comes with the cap on the LGA1151 socket.  
• The product warranty does not cover damage to the socket contacts resulting from incorrect CPU installation/removal, or misplacement/loss/incorrect removal of the PnP cap.

To install the CPU:

<PIC>

1. Press the CPU socket lever down and move it outward to release it from the retention tab.

<PIC>

2. Open the socket cover.  
3. Align the triangle orientation marks on the CPU Installation Tool and the CPU.

<PIC>

4. Secure the CPU in the installation tool.  
5. Place it into the motherboard socket.  
6. Lower the load plate and lever.  
7. Remove the socket’s protective cover.

Additional notes:
• Ensure that the CPU is firmly clicked into place before installing it onto the CPU socket on the motherboard.  
• Use the CPU Installation Tool for installing the CPU only. DO NOT damage or bend the CPU Installation Tool.

You can know the system memory of this motherboard from the specifications, the DIMM slot layout, the recommended memory configuration, and the BIOS information.

<PIC>

This motherboard has 4 x DIMM slots and supports up to 64 GB of DDR4 memory, ranging from 2133 MHz up to overclocked 3400 MHz.

<PIC>

The motherboard comes with four Double Data Rate 4 (DDR4) DIMM sockets: DIMM_A1, DIMM_A2, DIMM_B1, and DIMM_B2. A DDR4 module is notched differently from DDR, DDR2, or DDR3 modules. DO NOT install a DDR, DDR2, or DDR3 memory module to the DDR4 slot.

<PIC>

You may install 2 GB, 4 GB, 8 GB, and 16 GB unbuffered non-ECC DDR4 DIMMs into the DIMM sockets. Recommended installation:
• 1 DIMM: install into DIMM_A2 for better compatibility.
• 2 DIMMs: install into DIMM_A2 and DIMM_B2 for better compatibility.
• 4 DIMMs: install into DIMM_A1, DIMM_A2, DIMM_B1, and DIMM_B2.

<PIC>

You can also check memory information in BIOS. Enter the ASUS UEFI BIOS Utility Advanced Mode, then open the Main menu. The Main menu provides an overview of basic system information, including memory information.

Notes:
• DIMM voltage below 1.65 V is recommended to protect the CPU.
• If you install 4 GB or more memory, a 64-bit operating system is recommended for effective memory use.
• For optimum compatibility, install memory modules with the same CAS Latency and preferably from the same vendor/version.,['Manual25_2', 'Manual25_18', 'Manual25_19', 'Manual25_78']

The Serial port connector (10-1 pin COM) is an internal motherboard connector for a serial (COM) port. Connect the serial port module cable to this connector, then install the module to a slot opening at the back of the system chassis.

<PIC>

Serial port (COM) connector

The COM module is purchased separately.

In BIOS, you can configure it under Serial Port Configuration:
- Serial Port [On]: enables or disables the serial port (COM). Options: [On] [Off]
- Change Settings [IO=3F8h; IRQ=4]: appears only when Serial Port is [On], and allows you to select the Serial Port base address. Options: [IO=3F8h; IRQ=4], [IO=2F8h; IRQ=3], [IO=3E8h; IRQ=4], [IO=2E8h; IRQ=3],['Manual25_39']

The TPM connector (14-1 pin TPM) supports a Trusted Platform Module (TPM) system, which securely store keys, digital certificates, passwords and data. A TPM system also helps enhance the network security, protects digital identities, and ensures platform integrity.

<PIC>

TPM connector,['Manual25_40']

The sealing ring is part of the pressure cooking lid, located on the underside of the lid around the sealing ring rack.

<PIC>

When the pressure cooking lid is closed, the sealing ring creates an air-tight seal between the lid and the inner pot. The sealing ring must be installed before using the cooker. Only one sealing ring should be installed in the lid at a time.

<PIC>

Tip: Silicone is porous, so it absorbs strong aromas and certain flavors. Keep extra sealing rings on hand to limit the transfer of aromas and flavors between dishes.

CAUTION:
Only use authorized Multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer sealing rings. DO NOT use a stretched or damaged sealing ring.
· Always check for cuts, deformation and correct installation of the sealing ring before cooking.
· Sealing rings stretch over time with normal use. The sealing ring should be replaced every 12-18 months or sooner if you notice stretching, deformation or damage.
Failure to follow these instructions may cause food to discharge, which may lead to personal injury or property damage.

To remove the sealing ring:
Grip the edge of the silicone and pull the sealing ring out from behind the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack. With the sealing ring removed, inspect the steel rack to make sure it is secured centered, and an even height all the way around the lid. Do not attempt to repair a deformed sealing ring rack.

<PIC>

To install the sealing ring:
Place the sealing ring over the sealing ring rack and press it into place. Press down firmly to prevent puckering. When installed properly, the sealing ring is snug behind sealing ring rack and should not fall out when the lid is turned over.

<PIC>

If steam leaks from the side of the lid, check the sealing ring: it may need to be installed, replaced, or cleaned. Also make sure the lid is properly closed. If the sealing ring rack is warped or the inner pot rim is misshapen, contact Customer Care.

The steam release valve is located on the top of the pressure cooking lid.

<PIC>

The quick release button controls the steam release valve — the part controls whether pressure is released or not. It has two positions: Seal position and Vent position.

<PIC>

The steam release valve sits loosely on the steam release pipe. When the cooker releases pressure, steam ejects from the top of the steam release valve. The steam release valve is integral to product safety and necessary for pressure cooking.

<PIC>

For Quick Release, press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into the Vent position. When pressed down, a jet of steam ejects through the top of the steam release valve. This is normal.

<PIC>

Important safety notes:
Do not cover or block the steam release valve in any way.
Do not lean over or place your hands or face over the steam release valve when the appliance is in operation or has residual pressure.
Pressurized steam releases through the top of the steam release valve. Keep exposed skin away from the steam release valve.

Care and maintenance:
To remove the steam release valve, pull the steam release valve up and off the steam release pipe. The steam release valve must be installed before using the cooker.

<PIC>

To install the steam release valve, place the steam release valve on the steam release pipe and press down firmly. The steam release valve sits loosely on the steam release pipe when installed properly but should remain in place when the lid is turned over.

For Natural Release, leave the quick release button in the popped-up Seal position after cooking completes and turn Keep Warm off. Depressurization time varies and can take up to 40 minutes. The float valve will drop into the lid once the pressure is fully released.

When pressed down, a jet of steam ejects through the top of the steam release valve. This is normal. When pressure has been fully released, the float valve drops into the lid.

Natural Release (NR or NPR) means cooking stops gradually. As the temperature within the cooker drops, the cooker depressurizes naturally over time.

<PIC>

What to do:
Leave the quick release button in the popped-up “Seal position” after cooking completes and turn Keep Warm off.

What to expect:
The meal continues to cook, also called “carryover cooking” or “resting”. Depressurization time varies and can take up to 40 minutes. When the pressure is fully released, the float valve will drop into the lid.

Notice:
Use NR to depressurize the cooker after cooking high-starch foods, such as soups, stews, chilis, pasta, oatmeal and congee, or after cooking foods that expand when cooked, such as beans and grains.

Safety note:
Do not attempt to remove the lid while the float valve is up, and never force the lid open. The float valve must be down before attempting to remove the lid.,['Manual30_9']

The two primary modes are:

<PIC>

2. Virtual Wall Mode: When the switch is in the "up" position, the device functions as a virtual wall. It can block openings of up to 10 feet by creating an invisible, cone-shaped barrier that only the vacuum can see.

To clean the extractors (brushes or rollers), follow these steps:

1. Pinch the yellow extractor frame release tabs, lift up the extractor frame and remove any obstructions.  
<PIC>

2. Remove the extractors and remove the yellow extractor caps. Remove any hair or debris that has collected underneath the caps and around the metal axles. Reinstall the extractor caps.

The machine is stopped by braking the entire drive system. Squeeze the brake lever towards the handlebar to stop the machine. When the brake is operated, the brake light will illuminate.

Before using a snowmobile, perform these essential preparation checks:

1. Read the Owner’s Manual and all labels before operating the vehicle. Become familiar with all controls and their functions.

2. Check fuel: make sure there is sufficient fuel in the tank, and make sure the fuel tank cap is closed securely after refueling. Do not allow snow or ice to enter the fuel tank.
<PIC>

3. Check engine oil: make sure there is sufficient oil in the oil tank.
<PIC>

4. Check throttle operation: be sure the throttle lever moves freely and returns to the idle position when released.
<PIC>

5. Check the manual starter: inspect the starter rope for damage or abnormal condition before starting.
<PIC>

6. Check the brake and steering for proper operation before starting the engine. Apply the parking brake before starting, but never drive with the parking brake applied.
<PIC>

7. Check the drive system and running parts: V-belt, drive V-belt guard, drive track, slide runners, ski/ski runner, and fittings/fasteners. Do not operate the machine if the drive track is damaged.
<PIC>

8. Check lights, battery, service tools, and spare parts. It is good practice to carry tools, spare parts, and for long-distance trips, extra fuel and oil.

9. Wear protective clothing: an approved helmet, face shield or goggles, snowmobile suit, boots, and snowmobile gloves or mittens.
<PIC>

Important: Do not operate the machine after drinking alcohol or taking drugs, do not run the engine indoors, and do not allow anyone to stand behind the machine when starting, inspecting, or adjusting it.,['Manual34_93', 'Manual34_96', 'Manual34_97', 'Manual34_98', 'Manual34_108', 'Manual34_107', 'Manual34_11']

Before starting, be sure to check “SAFETY INFORMATION” carefully and be sure the parking brake is applied. Also, be sure the engine stop switch is in the “ON” position.

<PIC>

1. Fully open the starter lever (choke).
   - Starter lever (choke)
   - Fully open: cold engine starting
   - Half-open: warm engine up
   - Close: warm engine starting

2. For a manual starting model, turn the main switch to the “ON” position.

<PIC>

Pull slowly on the recoil starter until it is engaged, then pull it briskly.

<PIC>

After the engine starts, put the starter lever (choke) in the “Half-open” position. Warm up the engine until it does not run roughly or begin to stall when the starter lever is returned to the “Close” position.

3. For an electric starting model, turn the main switch to the “START” position.

<PIC>

After the engine starts, put the starter lever (choke) in the “Half-open” position. Warm up the engine until it does not run roughly or begin to stall when the starter lever is returned to the “Close” position.

CAUTION:
Release the switch immediately after the engine starts. If the engine fails to start, release the switch, wait a few seconds, then try again. Each attempt should be as short as possible to preserve the battery. Do not crank the engine more than 10 seconds on any one attempt.,['Manual34_111', 'Manual34_115', 'Manual34_116', 'Manual34_117']

Before operating the snowmobile, check the throttle, brake, and steering for proper operation before starting the engine. The steering handlebar and steering ski are the key parts used to control direction.

<PIC>

To ride and steer properly:
1. Become familiar with the snowmobile at slow speeds first.
2. Always keep both hands on the handlebars.
3. Turn the handlebar in the desired direction.
4. When approaching a curve, slow down and begin to turn the handlebars in the desired direction.
5. Put your weight on the running board to the inside of the turn and lean your upper body into the turn. This “body English” is the key to turning on most snow surfaces.

<PIC>

Pre-operation steering check:
Check the steering system for excessive play before riding. If there is abnormal looseness or movement at the handlebars, steering column, or skis, do not operate the snowmobile until it is inspected or repaired.

<PIC>

Important cautions:
Avoid higher speeds until you are thoroughly familiar with the operation of your snowmobile. Improper riding procedures, abrupt throttle changes, excessive braking, incorrect body movements, or too much speed for the sharpness of the turn may cause the snowmobile to tip.,['Manual34_13', 'Manual34_127', 'Manual34_108']

For most snow surfaces, “body English” is the key to turning.

As you approach a curve, slow down and begin to turn the handlebars in the desired direction. As you do so, put your weight on the running board to the inside of the turn and lean your upper body into the turn.

<PIC>

Practice this procedure at slow speed many times in a large flat area with no obstacles. Once learned, you can perform it at higher speeds or in tighter curves. Lean more as the turn gets sharper or is made at higher speeds.

If your snowmobile begins to tip while turning, lean more into the turn to regain balance. If necessary, gradually let off on the throttle and/or steer to the outside of the turn.

Remember: Avoid higher speeds until you are thoroughly familiar with the operation of your snowmobile.,['Manual34_127']

To ride uphill properly on a snowmobile:

<PIC>

You should practice first on gentle slopes. Try more difficult climbs only after you have developed your skill. As you approach a hill, accelerate before you start the climb, and then reduce the throttle opening to prevent track slippage.

It is also important to keep your weight on the uphill side at all times. On climbs straight up the hill, this can be accomplished by leaning forward and, on steeper inclines, standing on the running boards and leaning forward over the handlebars.

Slow down as you reach the crest of the hill, and be prepared to react to obstacles, sharp drops, or other people or vehicles which may be on the other side.

If you are unable to continue up a hill, do not spin the track. Stop the engine and set the parking brake. Then pull the rear of the snowmobile around to point the machine back down the hill. Do not get on the downhill side of the machine. When the snowmobile is pointed downhill, restart the engine, release the parking brake, and descend the hill.

<PIC>

When crossing a slope or side hill, lean your body to position your weight towards the uphill side. A recommended riding position is to kneel with the knee of the downhill leg on the seat and the foot of the uphill leg on the running board.

WARNING: Side hills and steep slopes are not recommended for a beginner or novice snowmobiler.,['Manual34_127', 'Manual34_129']

When riding downhill, keep speed to a minimum. It is important to apply just enough throttle to keep the clutch engaged while descending the hill. This will allow you to use engine compression to help slow the machine, and to keep the snowmobile from rolling freely down the hill. Also apply the brake frequently, with light pressure.

<PIC>

WARNING: Use extra caution when applying the brake during descent. Excessive braking will cause the track to lock and will cause a loss of control.,['Manual34_128']

Crossing the face of a slope (side hill) requires you to properly position your weight to maintain proper balance. As you travel across the slope, lean your body to position your weight towards the uphill side.

A recommended riding position is to kneel with the knee of the downhill leg on the seat and the foot of the uphill leg on the running board. This position will make it easier for you to shift your body weight as needed.

<PIC>

Snow and ice are slippery, so be prepared for the possibility that your snowmobile could begin to slip sideways on the slope. If this happens, steer in the direction of the slide if there are no obstacles in your path. As you regain proper balance, gradually steer again in the direction you wish to travel.

If your snowmobile starts to tip, steer down the hill to regain balance.

Warning: Side hills are not recommended for a beginner or novice snowmobiler. If you are unable to maintain correct balance, and your snowmobile is going to tip over, dismount your snowmobile immediately on the uphill side.,['Manual34_129']

Steps to inspect the spark plug on a snowmobile:

1. Remove the spark plug periodically for inspection, because heat and deposits will cause it to slowly break down and erode.

2. Check the coloration on the white porcelain insulator around the center electrode. The ideal coloration is a medium to light tan color for a machine being ridden normally. If the spark plug shows a distinctly different color, there could be something wrong with the engine. Do not attempt to diagnose such problems yourself; take the machine to your dealer.

3. Make sure the spark plug has the proper thread length, also called reach. If the reach is too long, overheating and engine damage may result. If it is too short, spark plug fouling, poor performance, carbon buildup, hot spots, and thread damage may result.

<PIC>

4. Before installing any spark plug, measure the electrode gap with a wire thickness gauge and adjust it to specification.

<PIC>

5. When installing the plug, always clean the gasket surface. Wipe off any grime from the threads and tighten the spark plug properly.

Spark plug torque: 28 Nm (2.8 m-kg, 20 ft-lb)

Note: Consult your dealer before changing to a different type of spark plug.,['Manual34_138', 'Manual34_139']

To clean a snowmobile:

1. Thoroughly clean the machine, inside and out, to remove corrosive salts and acids that can accumulate.
2. Use Mud and Grease Release, or an equivalent, to loosen mud, grease and grime.
3. Wash with mild soap.
4. Rinse completely.
5. Dry completely.

Note: If the snowmobile was transported in areas where road salt is used, be sure to clean the machine when you get to your destination to remove any corrosive salts.

Common causes and conditions that can lead to poor reception include:

1. Ignition interference  
Picture may flutter, drift, suffer from black spots or horizontal streaking. Usually caused by interference from automobile ignition systems, neon lamps, electrical drills and other electrical appliances.  
<PIC>

2. Ghosts / multiple images  
Ghosts are caused when the TV signal splits and follows two paths: one direct path and one reflected off tall buildings, hills or other objects. Changing the direction or position of the antenna may improve reception.  
<PIC>

3. Snow / weak signal area  
If the receiver is located in a weak, fringe area of a TV signal, the picture may be marred by small dots. It may be necessary to install a special antenna to improve the picture.  
<PIC>

Other possible causes include:
- Strong, random signals from a car or airplane interfering with the TV signal.
- The signal from the antenna is weak.
- Old, bad, or illegally recorded tapes being played.
- Station trouble or poor broadcast signal quality.
- Poor antenna connection or local interference.

For poor reception, the troubleshooting checklist recommends trying another channel, checking antenna connections, checking for local interference, adjusting the antenna, adjusting picture control, or programming channels again using AUTO PROGRAM.  
<PIC>,['television0_01', 'television0_02', 'television0_03', 'Manual35_45']

For the best reception, use an outdoor antenna. Severely weathered antennas and antenna cables can reduce the signal quality, so inspect them before connecting.

<PIC>

Steps:

1. Choose the correct antenna cable type:
   - 300 Ohm Flat Wire: Connect the 300 ohm flat wire to screws on the 300 ohm to 75 ohm adapter. Then push the end of 300 ohm to 75 ohm adapter into the 75 ohm antenna jack.
   - 75 Ohm Coaxial Cable: Connect the 75 ohm coaxial cable directly to the 75 ohm antenna jack.

2. After the antenna is connected, make sure:
   - Your TV has been connected to an antenna.
   - Your TV has been plugged in a power outlet.

3. Memorize available channels using AUTO PROGRAM:
   - Press the AUTO PRG. button.
   - Press the AUTO PRG. button again to start.
   - If you want to stop auto programming, press the ENTER button. Only the channels searched up to that time are memorized.

4. If reception is poor:
   - Change the direction or position of the antenna to improve reception, especially if you see ghosts or multiple images.
   - If the programmed signal has poor quality, memorize again in Auto prog.
   - For unstable pictures such as horizontal stripes, twisted picture, or no color, use the Fine adjustment in the Manual channel menu.

Note: AUTO PROGRAM stores all receivable channels regardless of receiving signal. If the signal is weak, it may be necessary to install a special antenna to improve the picture.,['Manual35_39']

To properly connect a DVD player to the TV for best performance:

1. Connect the component DVD video outputs on the DVD player to the TV’s COMPONENT DVD INPUT (480i) ports:
Y → Y, Pb/PB → PB, Pr/PR → PR.

2. Connect the DVD player’s audio outputs to the TV’s audio input ports:
L → L audio input, R → R audio input.

<PIC>

Note: Some DVD players may label the component outputs differently. Match them as follows:
Y → Y  
Cb / B-Y / Pb / PB → PB  
Cr / R-Y / Pr / PR → PR

<PIC>

3. Turn the TV on, then press the TV/VIDEO button on the remote control or on the front panel to select COMPONENT.

4. Turn on the DVD player and start playback.

Note: This TV is programmed to remember which mode it was last set to, even if you turn the TV off.,['Manual35_43', 'Manual35_44']

To effectively troubleshoot technical issues or malfunctions, you should first verify that the device has a stable power supply and that all physical cables or wireless connections are properly secured. Next, check for any error codes, warning lights, or system messages that might indicate a specific fault. It is also important to ensure that the device software or firmware is updated to the latest version and to perform a basic restart or factory reset if applicable. Additionally, inspect the product for any visible physical damage or signs of overheating and confirm that it is being used within the environmental parameters specified in the user manual. If these steps do not resolve the issue, please provide the specific symptoms so we can offer more targeted assistance or initiate a formal repair process.

During depressurization, follow these safety precautions to ensure safe operation:

<PIC>

- Although cooking has finished, the pressure cooker remains pressurized and hot. The pressure must be reduced before removing the lid.
- DO NOT attempt to remove the lid while the float valve is up, and NEVER force the lid open. The float valve must be down before attempting to remove the lid.
- Steam ejected from the steam release valve is hot. DO NOT place hands, face, or exposed skin over the steam release valve when releasing pressure.
- DO NOT cover the steam release valve.
- Follow the recipe instructions to depressurize the cooker and use the proper venting method.
- Do not use Quick Release when cooking fatty, oily, thick, or high-starch foods such as stews, chilis, pasta, and congee, or foods that expand when cooked such as beans and grains.

Failure to follow these precautions may result in serious personal injury and/or property damage.,['Manual30_8']

Your power toothbrush has 3 intensity settings:

- High intensity: three lights
- Medium intensity: two lights
- Low intensity: one light

<PIC>

To manually select your desired intensity, press the intensity indicator lights on the handle to cycle through the options. The intensity setting can be changed before, during, or after brushing.

<PIC>

Note: The intensity setting can also be customized from the app. If Adaptive Intensity is active, the toothbrush may automatically lower the intensity by 1 level when you apply excess pressure for an extended period of time; every new brushing cycle will return to your pre-selected setting.,['toothbrush0_06', 'toothbrush0_07']

You can activate or deactivate the following features of your toothbrush:
- Adaptive Intensity
- Pressure Sensor Feedback
- Scrubbing Feedback
- Brush head replacement reminder

Note: Adaptive Intensity will be deactivated when pressure sensor is deactivated.

From app:
The following features can be activated or deactivated from the app:
- Adaptive Intensity
- Scrubbing Feedback

From handle:
Step 1: Place the handle on the charging stand.

Step 2: Press and hold power button for:

<PIC>

- Adaptive intensity: up to 3 seconds  
  1 beep → SenseIQ indicator lights up for 3 seconds

- Pressure Sensor Feedback: up to 5 seconds  
  1 beep and then 2 beeps → SenseIQ indicator and light ring lights up purple for 3 seconds

- Brush head replacement reminder: up to 7 seconds  
  1 beep, 2 beeps and then 3 beeps → Brush head replacement reminder indicator lights up for 3 seconds

<PIC>

Together with:
- If you see the battery indication blink white 3 times and hear 3 tones from low to high, then the feature has been activated.
- If you see battery indication blink amber 3 times and hear 3 tones high to low, then the feature has been deactivated.,['Manual37_13', 'toothbrush0_11']

To charge the toothbrush inside the travel case:

1. Plug the USB cord into the travel case and into the USB wall adapter.  
<PIC>

2. Plug the wall adapter into an electrical outlet.

3. Place toothbrush into travel case.  
<PIC>

To ensure charging has started successfully:
- The toothbrush will beep twice and the lights will illuminate in an upward motion.
- While charging, the battery indicator blinks in white.  
<PIC>

4. Leave travel case plugged in until toothbrush is fully charged. The battery light will turn off, stop blinking, when the handle has finished charging.  
<PIC>

Battery status while on the travel case:
- Charging: light blinks in white.
- Full: light illuminates white for 30 seconds and then turns off.  
<PIC>

Note: Make sure the travel case is placed on its side for better stability. Also, only use the USB wall adapter, charging base and charging travel case to charge the battery.,['Manual37_18', 'Manual37_19', 'Manual37_20', 'Manual37_21', 'Manual37_22']
